ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for stir-fried bitter gourd with egg; mapped to stir-fried food product as the closest defensible class. Bitter gourd (Momordica charantia) is the primary vegetable component.
ontology.foodex2_description: Bitter gourd (Momordica charantia) — used as the primary ingredient anchor; dish-level FoodEx2 code for this specific preparation is not available in the published FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP maps to bitter gourd as a raw commodity. No dish-level FoodEx2 code exists for stir-fried bitter gourd with egg; ingredient-level anchor used as the closest defensible match.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical side dish serving of stir-fried bitter gourd with egg is approximately 150 g, equivalent to a small bowl or side plate portion as served in a Singaporean hawker centre or home meal.

nutrient_source.title: Stir Fried Bittergourd with Egg — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Stir Fried Bittergourd with Egg (cr_id F14080035). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of bitter gourd and low-carbohydrate vegetable dishes — analogous estimate based on bitter gourd (Momordica charantia) GI data from peer-reviewed literature
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Peer-reviewed literature (Leung et al., 2009; Ooi et al., 2012)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried bitter gourd with egg as a composite dish. GI estimated at approximately 32 (low) based on: (1) bitter gourd (Momordica charantia) has a documented low GI (~32) per peer-reviewed studies including Leung et al. (2009) and Ooi et al. (2012) cited in the University of Sydney GI Database framework; (2) the dish contains only 1.6 g carbohydrate per 100 g, making the glycemic load negligible; (3) eggs contribute zero carbohydrate. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (32 × 1.6 × 1.5) / 100 = 0.77, rounded to 0.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 221 mg/100 g — borderline for a vegetable dish; high fat content (20.3 g/100 g) adds cardiovascular load
hypertension.guidance: Limit portion to 100–120 g and reduce soy sauce and added salt during cooking to bring sodium below 200 mg/100 g; the high fat content warrants portion control for individuals managing blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (1.6 g/100 g), zero sugar, and bitter gourd's evidence-based hypoglycaemic properties support blood glucose stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to negligible carbohydrate and the hypoglycaemic bioactives in bitter gourd (charantin, polypeptide-p); monitor for additive hypoglycaemic effects if on insulin or oral hypoglycaemic agents, and note that high fat content may slow gastric emptying and alter post-meal glucose kinetics.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (20.3 g/100 g) and cholesterol (185 mg/100 g) from eggs and cooking oil; saturated fat is moderate at 3.5 g/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Consume in moderate portions (100–120 g) as the high fat and cholesterol content from eggs and stir-fry oil may contribute to LDL elevation; bitter gourd's antioxidant and lipid-modulating properties provide partial offset, and saturated fat remains below the 5 g/100 g threshold.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Bitter gourd provides vitamin C (~25 mg/100 g) and antioxidants that reduce oxidative stress; saturated fat is below 5 g/100 g threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients; reduce soy sauce to lower sodium and decrease risk of RA-related tissue swelling, and monitor lipid levels if on corticosteroids given the cholesterol content from eggs.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Bitter Gourd with Egg (苦瓜炒蛋; Peria goreng dengan telur) is a classic Singaporean Chinese home-cooked and hawker dish in which sliced bitter gourd (Momordica charantia) is stir-fried at high heat with beaten eggs, garlic, and aromatics in cooking oil, typically seasoned with soy sauce and salt. The dish has a characteristic bitter, slightly astringent flavour from the gourd balanced by the richness of egg and the savouriness of soy sauce. The texture contrasts the slight crunch of the gourd with the soft, custardy egg. Nutritionally, the dish is high in fat (20.3 g/100 g) primarily from eggs and cooking oil, with moderate protein (10.05 g/100 g) from egg, and very low carbohydrate (1.6 g/100 g) with zero sugar, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns. Sodium is borderline at 221 mg/100 g and should be moderated for hypertension management. Bitter gourd contains bioactive compounds including charantin, polypeptide-p, and vicine that have evidence-based hypoglycaemic properties, making this dish particularly relevant for Type 2 Diabetes dietary management. The dish also provides vitamin C (~25 mg/100 g) and antioxidants. It is vegetarian, halal, and dairy-free. A typical serving is approximately 150 g as a side dish in a Singaporean meal.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F14080035) at confidence 0.95. The HPB is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. All seven reported nutrient fields are from this exact entry. Secondary nutrient values (saturated fat, cholesterol, vitamin C, potassium) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for bitter gourd and egg as supporting references, documented in notes.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this specific stir-fried preparation is not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry F14080035, and no sufficiently close equivalent entry exists in USDA FoodData Central for this composite dish as prepared. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this specific preparation is not reported in the HPB source. 3. calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg are set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this dish and detailed micronutrient data for this specific preparation is not available in the HPB or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases as of 2026. 4. saturated_fat_g (3.5 g/100 g), cholesterol_mg (185 mg/100 g), vitamin_c_mg (25 mg/100 g), and potassium_mg (180 mg/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for bitter gourd (USDA FDC ID 169928) and scrambled/fried egg (USDA FDC ID 173424) in proportions consistent with the dish composition, as these fields are not explicitly reported in the HPB entry. 5. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0: confirmed zero for this home-style stir-fried preparation using standard cooking oil; no hydrogenated fats are used. 6. sugar_g is confirmed zero per HPB source. 7. GI is an analogous estimate (~32, low) based on bitter gourd GI data from peer-reviewed literature; no direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. 8. The dietary tag 'plant_based' in the original input has been removed as the dish contains egg and is therefore not plant-based; it is correctly classified as vegetarian. 9. Gluten-free flag is set to false as soy sauce typically contains wheat; a gluten-free variant would require tamari.
